summary: This project bridges the abstract elegance of network topology with the lived human experience of visual perception. It aligns with my interest in how mathematical structures underpin cultural and biological phenomena, specifically asking whether the model captures the narrative
body: Imagine a student tracing the veins on their own hand, marveling at the branching pattern, before we ask them to map the topology of the brain. There is a quiet beauty in trying to quantify the very structures that allow us to be fooled by sight. This proposal does not merely measure susceptibility; it invites us to consider the shape of perception itself. I am reminded of the work on topological data analysis in brain networks, where the shape of the data matters as much as the signal itself. Does the model account for the 'texture' of the illusion, or just the frequency of the error? If the network topology truly correlates with susceptibility, we are essentially mapping the geometry of the mind's vulnerability. Push forward, but keep the human story in the center of the equation.

summary: This project asks the right question—how brain network topology shapes perception—but the validation document does not yet specify which synaptic or circuit-level mechanisms would mediate the observed individual differences. In my work with Aplysia, we learned that behavior canno
body:
Your research question is well-formulated, but I must ask: at what level of explanation will you anchor your findings? You propose that resting-state network topology predicts susceptibility to visual illusions, which is a compelling hypothesis. However, in my own research on the cellular basis of memory, we found that every cognitive claim must eventually be traced back to synaptic change—otherwise we are describing correlation without mechanism.

The validation document mentions 'individual variation in resting-state brain network topology' but does not specify which circuit parameters will be measured. Will you examine structural connectivity via diffusion MRI, or functional connectivity via fMRI? More importantly, will you consider how individual differences in synaptic plasticity genes (such as CREB or BDNF polymorphisms) might contribute to both network architecture and perceptual susceptibility? These molecular factors were central to my work on long-term memory consolidation, and I would urge you to consider them here.

Furthermore, I would ask you to define more precisely what you mean by 'susceptibility to visual illusions.' Will you use the Müller-Lyer, Ebbinghaus, or motion illusions? Each engages different neural circuits—the motion aftereffect, for instance, involves V5/MT and adaptation at the synaptic level. Without this specificity, your results risk remaining at the level of network topology without the cellular grounding that would make the findings truly explanatory.

I have spent my career arguing that psychiatry, psychology, and molecular neuroscience are one science viewed at different magnifications. This project has the potential to advance that program, but only if it commits to the full ladder of explanation—from behavior to synapse to molecule.
